content.text: Understanding how an algorithm's resource consumption grows with input size is one of the most transferable skills in software engineering. Whether designing a retrieval pipeline, processing conversation history, or planning multi-step agent workflows, the same mathematical framework — Big-O notation — reveals which approaches remain viable at scale and which quietly collapse under production load.
What it is
Algorithmic complexity

is a formal characterisation of how an algorithm's time or memory requirements grow as a function of input size. It answers the question: if the input doubles, does the work double, quadruple, or stay roughly flat? The standard vocabulary for this question uses
Big-O notation
, written
, where
n
represents the input size and
f(n)
is a simple mathematical function describing the dominant growth term.
Big-O is an
asymptotic upper bound
: it describes worst-case growth, ignores constant factors, and drops lower-order terms. A function that takes
steps is still
O(n)

because the 3 and 47 become irrelevant when n is large. This intentional imprecision makes Big-O a practical engineering tool rather than a precise performance predictor — it identifies the structural shape of an algorithm's scaling behaviour, independent of hardware, language, or implementation details.
The standard complexity classes
Six classes cover the vast majority of algorithms encountered in practice:
O(1) — Constant
: Work does not grow with input. Hash table lookup, stack push/pop, array index access.
O(log n) — Logarithmic
: Work grows by one unit each time input doubles. Binary search, balanced BST lookup, heap operations.
O(n) — Linear
: Work grows proportionally. Scanning all messages, summing a list, linear search.
O(n log n) — Linearithmic
: Slightly super-linear. Efficient comparison sorts (merge sort, Timsort), heap sort.
O(n²) — Quadratic
: Every pair of elements is examined. Bubble sort, naive duplicate detection, all-pairs similarity.
O(2^n) — Exponential
: Work doubles with each additional element. Exhaustive subsets, naive recursive Fibonacci, brute-force planning.
Beyond these,
O(n!)
(factorial) appears in naive travelling-salesman solutions, and
O(√n)
occasionally appears in number theory algorithms. A second dimension,
space complexity
, applies the same notation to memory rather than time — an algorithm can be fast but have an unacceptable memory footprint at scale.
Why it matters

Most algorithms work well on a developer's laptop with synthetic test data. The failure mode is production scale. An agent that re-reads all 50,000 tool descriptions on every turn performs an O(n) scan 50,000 times per session. An embedding pipeline that compares every document to every other document to find duplicates runs O(n²) comparisons — fine for 100 docs, unusable for 100,000.

Complexity analysis surfaces these problems before they appear in production monitoring. It also guides technology selection: when a workload requires thousands of membership checks, knowing that Python
list.__contains__
is O(n) and
set.__contains__

is O(1) average is directly actionable. When planning a retrieval step over a million-chunk corpus, knowing that exhaustive cosine comparison is O(n) per query while an HNSW index is approximately O(log n) makes the architecture decision obvious.

For agentic AI systems specifically, complexity matters at three levels: within a single agent turn (context assembly, retrieval, reranking), across a session (memory management, tool caching), and across the fleet (concurrent sessions multiplied by per-session cost). A quadratic operation that is tolerable at n=100 messages becomes catastrophic when n=10,000 conversation turns accumulate across a long-running task.
Benefits
Technology-independent prediction
: Complexity analysis holds across languages and hardware generations. An O(n²) algorithm on a machine that is 100× faster is still slower than an O(n log n) algorithm once n is large enough.
Algorithm comparison
: Two algorithms that produce identical results can be compared rigorously. The one with lower complexity is always preferred at large n, regardless of the constant factor.
Early architectural signals
: Identifying that a planned pipeline component has quadratic complexity during design prevents expensive rewrites after deployment.
Budget estimation
: Knowing that a nightly batch job is O(n log n) in document count lets teams estimate compute cost as the corpus grows, without running the job at target scale first.
Debugging at scale
: When production latency suddenly increases non-linearly with load, complexity analysis directs investigation to the right algorithm rather than tuning infrastructure.
Communication
: Big-O gives engineers a shared vocabulary. 'This is O(n²) — we need to rethink it' is unambiguous; 'this is slow' is not.
Trade-offs

Big-O analysis abstracts away constants, and those constants matter. An O(log n) algorithm with a large constant factor (e.g., because it requires a network round-trip or a lock acquisition) may be slower in practice than an O(n) algorithm operating on data already in L1 cache. For very small n — say, fewer than a few hundred elements — the simpler O(n) algorithm is often faster because setup overhead dominates.
Worst vs. average case

: Big-O conventionally describes the worst case. Python's dict lookup is O(1) on average but O(n) in the (rare) worst case due to hash collisions. Algorithms like quicksort are O(n²) worst-case but O(n log n) average-case. The right bound depends on the attacker model and data distribution.
Space–time trade-off

: Caching results to achieve O(1) repeated lookups consumes memory. Memoising an exponential recursion trades O(2^n) time for O(n) memory. There is no free optimisation — faster usually means more space, or pre-computation time.
Amortised vs. worst-case per operation

: Python list append is O(1) amortised but occasionally O(n) when the backing array resizes. Designing for worst-case per operation can over-provision resources. Amortised analysis gives a more realistic picture for data-structure operations.
Complexity class vs. wall-clock performance

: Network I/O, database queries, and LLM API calls often dominate agentic pipelines by orders of magnitude. An O(1) hash lookup that still awaits a 300 ms API call is dominated by that latency, not its algorithmic complexity.
Premature optimisation risk
: Replacing a readable O(n) scan with a complex O(log n) data structure for data that never exceeds n=20 adds maintenance burden for no practical gain.
How it works
Counting operations
The formal process is: identify the
elementary operation
(comparison, assignment, memory access), count how many times it executes as a function of n, then keep only the dominant term and drop constants. For a single loop over n elements:
For nested loops, multiply the bounds of each loop. Two nested loops each iterating over n elements yields:
Sequential (non-nested) blocks add: an O(n) pass followed by an O(n log n) sort gives O(n + n log n) = O(n log n) because the dominant term wins.
Recurrence relations
Recursive algorithms are analysed with recurrence relations and solved with the Master Theorem. Binary search halves the problem each step:
Merge sort splits into two halves and merges (O(n) work) at each level:
Analysing loops in practice

# Rule 1: Sequential blocks — take the maximum complexity
def process(messages):            # O(n) — one pass
    for msg in messages:
        tokenise(msg)

    messages.sort(key=lambda m: m['ts'])  # O(n log n) — sort

    # Total: O(n log n)  (n log n dominates n)


# Rule 2: Nested loops — multiply bounds
def pairwise_similarity(docs):   # O(n^2) — all pairs
    results = []
    for i, a in enumerate(docs):         # n iterations
        for j, b in enumerate(docs):     # n iterations each
            if i != j:
                results.append(cosine(a, b))
    return results


# Rule 3: Early exit doesn't change worst-case
def find_first(items, target):   # O(n) worst-case
    for item in items:
        if item == target:
            return item          # may return early
    return None
Space complexity

Space complexity counts the additional memory an algorithm needs beyond its input. An in-place sort uses O(1) extra space; merge sort uses O(n) auxiliary space for the merge buffer. Memoisation of an n-deep recursion stores O(n) results. Storing the full conversation history of a session with m messages and average t tokens each consumes O(mt) memory — relevant when designing multi-session agent servers with thousands of concurrent users.
Architecture

The chart below illustrates how the six primary complexity classes diverge as input size n grows from 0 to 100. The vertical separation at n=100 between O(1) and O(2^n) spans many orders of magnitude — a gap that in practice translates from microseconds to heat-death-of-the-universe timeframes.

attrs.alt: Big-O complexity growth curves: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n²), O(2^n) plotted against input size n

content.text: The chart encodes a practical engineering heuristic: any algorithm that sits in the orange (O(n log n)) band or below is typically deployable at production scale. Algorithms in the red (O(n²)) band require careful input caps or approximation. Anything in the purple (O(2^n)) band is only viable when n is tightly bounded — often single digits.
When to use it
Always analyse
Hot paths
: Any code that executes on every agent turn: context assembly, tool dispatch, memory retrieval.
Data at scale
: Any loop whose iteration count is user-controlled or grows with corpus size.
Algorithm selection
: Before choosing between data structures (list vs. set, sorted list vs. heap, dict vs. trie).
Batch jobs
: Nightly embedding pipelines, re-indexing, log analysis — where n may be millions.
When not to over-invest
Prototype stage with n < 500
: Constant factors dominate; the simplest code is usually correct to ship first.
I/O-dominated code
: If each iteration makes a database or API call, the algorithmic complexity of in-process computation is irrelevant next to I/O latency.
One-time setup code
: Building an index at startup that is then queried millions of times. The O(n) setup cost is amortised to near-zero per query.
How to implement
Applying complexity analysis to a codebase is a four-step discipline:
Identify the input variable(s)
n. In agentic systems this is often: number of messages, number of documents, number of tool calls, or number of concurrent sessions.
Trace the hot path
— the code executed on every request. Count loop depths.
Apply the addition and multiplication rules
to derive T(n), then keep only the dominant term.
Replace high-complexity structures
with lower-complexity alternatives where beneficial (set for membership, dict for lookup, heap for top-k).

# Step-by-step refactor: tool registry lookup
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
# BEFORE: tools stored as a list — O(n) search per call
tools_list = [
    {"name": "search", "fn": do_search},
    {"name": "calculator", "fn": do_calc},
    # ... potentially hundreds of tools
]

def dispatch_v1(tool_name: str, args: dict):
    for tool in tools_list:          # O(n) — scans every tool
        if tool["name"] == tool_name:
            return tool["fn"](args)
    raise ValueError(f"Unknown tool: {tool_name}")


# AFTER: tools stored in a dict — O(1) lookup
tools_dict = {t["name"]: t["fn"] for t in tools_list}  # O(n) once at startup

def dispatch_v2(tool_name: str, args: dict):
    if tool_name not in tools_dict:  # O(1) average
        raise ValueError(f"Unknown tool: {tool_name}")
    return tools_dict[tool_name](args)


# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Membership test: deduplication across sessions
seen_ids_list: list[str] = []         # O(n) per check
seen_ids_set: set[str] = set()        # O(1) average per check

def record_v1(doc_id: str) -> bool:
    if doc_id in seen_ids_list:        # O(n) — list scan
        return False
    seen_ids_list.append(doc_id)
    return True

def record_v2(doc_id: str) -> bool:
    if doc_id in seen_ids_set:         # O(1) — hash lookup
        return False
    seen_ids_set.add(doc_id)
    return True
Pre-building an index (the
dict
comprehension above) is an O(n) one-time cost at startup. Every subsequent dispatch benefits from O(1) average lookup. This pattern —
preprocess once, query many times
— is the core idiom for converting linear scans into constant-time operations.
Examples
Example 1 — context window trimming (Python)
An agent must keep conversation history below a token budget. The naive approach recomputes all token counts on every turn:

import heapq
from functools import lru_cache

# ── Naive: O(m) per turn (acceptable), but wrong if token_count itself is O(t) ──
def trim_naive(messages: list[dict], max_tokens: int) -> list[dict]:
    """Remove oldest messages until within budget. O(m) per call."""
    total = sum(len(m["content"].split()) for m in messages)  # rough token proxy
    while total > max_tokens and messages:
        removed = messages.pop(0)           # O(m) pop from front — use deque!
        total -= len(removed["content"].split())
    return messages


# ── Better: use collections.deque for O(1) pop from front ──
from collections import deque

def trim_efficient(messages: deque, max_tokens: int, token_counts: deque) -> deque:
    """
    Maintain a parallel deque of cached token counts.
    Both pop operations are O(1).
    Total complexity: O(k) where k = number of messages removed.
    """
    total = sum(token_counts)
    while total > max_tokens and messages:
        total -= token_counts.popleft()     # O(1)
        messages.popleft()                  # O(1)
    return messages


# ── Worst mistake: sort then slice every turn — O(m log m) unnecessarily ──
def trim_wrong(messages: list[dict], max_tokens: int) -> list[dict]:
    messages = sorted(messages, key=lambda m: m["ts"])   # O(m log m) — pointless
    # ... then trim from front
    return messages
Example 2 — top-k retrieval with a heap (Python)
Reranking n retrieved documents to find the top-k is a classic place where complexity choice matters:

import heapq
import time
import random

def generate_docs(n: int) -> list[dict]:
    return [{"id": i, "score": random.random()} for i in range(n)]

# Approach A: sort all n, slice top k  — O(n log n)
def top_k_sort(docs: list[dict], k: int) -> list[dict]:
    return sorted(docs, key=lambda d: d["score"], reverse=True)[:k]

# Approach B: heapq.nlargest — O(n log k)
def top_k_heap(docs: list[dict], k: int) -> list[dict]:
    return heapq.nlargest(k, docs, key=lambda d: d["score"])

# Benchmark for illustration
n, k = 100_000, 10
docs = generate_docs(n)

t0 = time.perf_counter()
top_k_sort(docs, k)
t1 = time.perf_counter()
top_k_heap(docs, k)
t2 = time.perf_counter()

print(f"sort:          {(t1-t0)*1000:.2f} ms  — O(n log n)")
print(f"heapq.nlargest:{(t2-t1)*1000:.2f} ms  — O(n log k)")
# With n=100_000 and k=10: heap is consistently faster because log(10) << log(100_000)
Example 3 — memoised Fibonacci to illustrate exponential vs. linear (JavaScript)
Real-world example

Consider a production RAG (retrieval-augmented generation) agent that serves 10,000 concurrent users, each with a session corpus of up to 5,000 document chunks. On every turn the agent must: retrieve the top-20 relevant chunks, assemble context within a 128k token window, and dispatch to the LLM.

The retrieval step uses an HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) approximate nearest-neighbour index with approximate O(log n) per query — acceptable at n=5,000. The alternative, exhaustive cosine similarity against all 5,000 chunks, would be O(n) per query: at 10,000 concurrent requests per second, the total compute is 5×10^7 similarity operations/second versus roughly 1.3×10^5 with the index — a 380× difference that determines whether this is a $5k/month or a $2M/month infrastructure bill.

# Simplified production retrieval with HNSW via hnswlib
import hnswlib
import numpy as np

DIM = 1536   # OpenAI ada-002 embedding dimension

def build_index(embeddings: np.ndarray) -> hnswlib.Index:
    """O(n log n) index build — done once per corpus update."""
    index = hnswlib.Index(space='cosine', dim=DIM)
    index.init_index(max_elements=len(embeddings), ef_construction=200, M=16)
    index.add_items(embeddings, np.arange(len(embeddings)))
    index.set_ef(50)   # query-time ef controls recall vs. speed tradeoff
    return index

def retrieve(index: hnswlib.Index, query_embedding: np.ndarray, k: int = 20):
    """Approximately O(log n) per query — scales to millions of chunks."""
    labels, distances = index.knn_query(query_embedding, k=k)
    return labels[0], distances[0]

# For n=5_000 chunks:
# Exhaustive: 5_000 cosine operations per query
# HNSW:       ~log2(5_000) ≈ 12 hops per query (approximate)

Context assembly after retrieval is O(k) where k=20 chunks — trivial. The session token-counting step runs O(t) where t is total tokens assembled — a single pass, unavoidable. The dominant cost is the LLM call itself, which is O(t) in the transformer's attention layer. Complexity analysis identified the retrieval step as the only component where algorithmic choice mattered — everything else was already near-linear or constant.
Common mistakes
Mistake 1 — list membership in an inner loop

# WRONG: O(n * m) — list 'in' is O(m) linear scan
allowed_ids = [...]          # list of m allowed doc IDs

for doc in retrieved_docs:   # n docs
    if doc["id"] in allowed_ids:  # O(m) — total: O(n*m)
        process(doc)

# CORRECT: convert to set once outside the loop
allowed_set = set(allowed_ids)   # O(m) once

for doc in retrieved_docs:       # n docs
    if doc["id"] in allowed_set: # O(1) average — total: O(n + m)
        process(doc)
Mistake 2 — rebuilding an index every call

# WRONG: sort happens on every tool dispatch — O(n log n) per call
def get_best_tool(tools: list, query: str) -> dict:
    scored = sorted(tools, key=lambda t: score(t, query))  # O(n log n) each call
    return scored[-1]

# CORRECT: score once, sort once, then binary-search or use a heap
import heapq

class ToolSelector:
    def __init__(self, tools):
        # O(n log n) one-time setup
        self._heap = None
        self._tools = tools

    def get_best(self, query: str) -> dict:
        # O(n) scoring + O(n log k) for top-1 — but still O(n) per query
        # For truly fast dispatch: pre-embed tools and use ANN index
        return heapq.nlargest(1, self._tools, key=lambda t: score(t, query))[0]
Mistake 3 — ignoring hidden O(n) in string concatenation

# WRONG: += on strings is O(k) per concatenation — total O(n^2) for n messages
context = ""
for msg in messages:        # n messages
    context += msg["text"]  # each += copies all previous chars — O(total_so_far)
# Final complexity: O(1 + 2 + ... + n) = O(n^2)

# CORRECT: collect then join — O(n) total
parts = [msg["text"] for msg in messages]  # O(n)
context = "\n".join(parts)                  # O(total_chars) — one allocation
Mistake 4 — confusing worst-case Big-O with typical runtime

Python's dict is described as O(1) average for get/set, but O(n) worst-case when all keys hash to the same bucket. In practice this is extremely rare with Python's hash randomisation, but for adversarial inputs (e.g., processing user-supplied keys) it can be triggered. Production systems that accept untrusted keys should be aware of this distinction.
Best practices
Profile before optimising
: Measure with cProfile, py-spy, or line_profiler before assuming which line is slow. The bottleneck is often surprising.
Choose the right data structure first
: Set for membership, dict for key lookup, heapq for priority queue, deque for efficient front-pops. Retrofitting these is expensive.
Cap all user-controlled n
: Every loop whose bound comes from user input is a potential DoS vector. Enforce max_messages, max_tools, max_docs at the API boundary.
Preprocess once, query many times
: Any data that is read repeatedly should be indexed once at load time. Build the dict, set, or ANN index in __init__ or on startup.
Use generators for large pipelines
: Transforming a million-row dataset with list comprehensions creates the full list in memory. Generators make the pipeline O(1) space.
Annotate complexity in docstrings
: Complex modules benefit from explicit complexity annotations so future maintainers know the contract they must preserve.
Test with realistic n
: Unit tests with n=10 never catch O(n²) bugs. Include at least one benchmark test with n=10,000 for every hot-path function.
Performance and scalability

In a single-process server, per-request complexity scales multiplicatively with concurrent request count. A function that is O(n) per request at 1,000 requests per second consumes O(1,000n) operations per second cluster-wide. This means that horizontal scaling (more servers) only defers, rather than solves, quadratic complexity: doubling servers halves per-server load, but user n keeps growing.
Approximate algorithms are a practical answer to intractable-complexity exact algorithms:
Approximate nearest neighbour (ANN)
: HNSW, FAISS IVF, ScaNN achieve sub-linear query complexity at the cost of small recall loss (typically 95–99% accuracy at 10–50× speedup over exact search).
Locality-sensitive hashing (LSH)
: Maps similar vectors to the same bucket with high probability; enables O(1) approximate similarity in the right regime.
Sampling
: Processing a random 10% sample of a corpus is O(n/10) — exact in expectation, with known statistical error bounds.
Streaming algorithms
: Count-Min Sketch for frequency estimation, HyperLogLog for cardinality — both O(1) space with small bounded error.

Token-level complexity for transformer-based LLMs deserves special attention: the standard self-attention mechanism is O(t²) in context length t, which is why context window quadrupling (from 4k to 128k tokens) required architectural innovations (flash attention, sliding window attention, RoPE) specifically to manage this quadratic wall. Application-level context management strategies — summarisation, retrieval, compression — are engineering responses to this fundamental algorithmic constraint.
Tooling and ecosystem
Profiling — finding where time actually goes

# cProfile — standard library profiler
import cProfile, pstats, io

def run_agent_turn(messages):
    """The function to profile."""
    pass  # your agent logic here

pr = cProfile.Profile()
pr.enable()
run_agent_turn(sample_messages)
pr.disable()

stream = io.StringIO()
ps = pstats.Stats(pr, stream=stream).sort_stats('cumulative')
ps.print_stats(20)          # top 20 functions by cumulative time
print(stream.getvalue())
Complexity-aware standard library tools
collections.deque
: O(1) append and popleft — use instead of list when queue semantics are needed.
heapq
: O(log n) push/pop, O(n log k) nlargest/nsmallest — use for priority queues and top-k.
collections.Counter
: O(n) construction, O(1) lookup — use for frequency maps.
bisect
: O(log n) binary search in a sorted list — use when you need insertion-order preservation with fast lookup.
functools.lru_cache / cache
: Memoisation with O(1) cache check — use to convert repeated O(f(n)) calls to O(1) after the first.
External tools
py-spy
: Sampling profiler that attaches to a running Python process without instrumentation — production-safe.
scalene
: Line-level profiler that attributes CPU, GPU, and memory to individual lines simultaneously.
FAISS / hnswlib / usearch
: ANN libraries that replace O(n) exhaustive vector search with approximately O(log n) indexed search.
Redis / Memcached
: O(1) average key–value lookup across processes — effective for shared tool registries or session caches.
Comparison with alternatives
Several related notations and methodologies exist alongside Big-O. Understanding when each is most appropriate prevents choosing the wrong tool for analysis:
Notation / Method
What it bounds
Use case
Limitation
Big-O — O(f(n))
Asymptotic upper bound (worst-case growth rate)
Algorithm selection; worst-case guarantees
Ignores constants; may be loose
Big-Theta — Θ(f(n))
Tight bound (both upper and lower)
Precise complexity classification
Harder to establish; often overkill
Big-Omega — Ω(f(n))
Asymptotic lower bound (best-case floor)
Proving no algorithm can do better (complexity theory)
Rarely used in applied engineering
Amortised analysis
Average cost per operation over a sequence
Data structures with occasional expensive ops (dynamic array, hash table resize)
Does not bound any single operation
Benchmarking / profiling
Actual wall-clock time at a specific n
Finding real bottlenecks; validating complexity predictions
Hardware-dependent; does not generalise
Little-o — o(f(n))
Strict upper bound (f strictly dominates)
Theoretical analysis; algorithm comparisons
Uncommon outside academic CS
Security and reliability
Algorithmic complexity intersects with security through
algorithmic complexity attacks

(also called ReDoS, HashDoS, or quadratic blowup attacks): an adversary supplies crafted input that forces a worst-case execution path, turning an O(1) average operation into O(n) or causing an O(n) algorithm to behave like O(n²) in practice. This can cause denial-of-service without exploiting any memory corruption.
Hash collision attacks (HashDoS)

: Crafted keys that all hash to the same bucket degrade dict/set to O(n). Python mitigates this with per-process hash randomisation (PYTHONHASHSEED). Avoid exposing raw hash-keyed structures to untrusted input without rate limiting.
ReDoS (Regular Expression Denial of Service)

: Certain regex patterns with backtracking exhibit O(2^n) time on adversarial inputs. Use linear-time regex engines (re2, Google's RE2) for untrusted patterns, or validate input length before matching.
Context stuffing
: A malicious user who can inject arbitrarily long messages forces O(t) and O(t²) attention costs in the LLM layer. Enforce hard max_tokens per message at the agent boundary.
Resource exhaustion via recursion
: Accepting user-defined tool chains or plan depths without a bound allows O(d) stack depth and potentially O(2^d) plan-space exploration. Always cap recursion/plan depth explicitly.

Reliability considerations centre on bounding n at the system boundary: all external inputs that influence loop bounds — message count, document count, tool count, plan depth — must be validated and capped before the hot path processes them. This is both a correctness principle (algorithms have design ranges) and a resilience principle (an agent that degrades gracefully under overload is more reliable than one that crashes).
Design principle

: Treat every external input that controls a loop bound as adversarial. Validate, cap, and log it before the loop runs. The cap should be the maximum n your complexity analysis has verified is safe at your performance budget.
Mental model
Think of Big-O as a
staffing forecast

for a library. If one librarian can serve one visitor in constant time regardless of how many books the library holds, that is O(1). If finding a book requires scanning every shelf, service time grows with the collection — O(n). If every visitor wants to borrow every book from every other visitor, work grows as the square of visitors — O(n²).

The forecast changes what you build. A library expecting 100 books can manage with a shelf-scanning librarian. A library expecting 10 million books must have a card catalogue (index) to remain functional. The library never changes its service promise — it always finds your book — but the mechanism must match the anticipated scale. Algorithmic complexity is that matching discipline: pick the mechanism whose growth rate the anticipated scale can sustain.
Key takeaways
Big-O describes scaling shape, not absolute speed
: it answers 'if n doubles, does work double or quadruple?' — not 'how many milliseconds does this take?'
Drop constants and lower-order terms
: O(3n + 47) simplifies to O(n); only the dominant term determines behaviour at large scale.
Nested loops multiply, sequential blocks add
: two nested n-loops give O(n²); two sequential n-loops give O(n). This single rule identifies most quadratic bugs.
Convert inner-loop linear scans to O(1) lookups
: replacing list membership tests with set or dict lookups is the single highest-value complexity optimisation in Python.
Space and time are traded, not eliminated
: memoisation, indexing, and caching all buy time-complexity reductions by spending memory.
Adversarial inputs can trigger worst-case paths
: HashDoS and ReDoS attacks exploit the gap between average-case and worst-case Big-O. Cap user-controlled n and sanitise inputs.
ANN indexes make vector search approximately O(log n)
: exhaustive search over large embedding corpora is O(n) and infeasible at scale; HNSW/FAISS/ScaNN make production RAG viable.
Profile before optimising, but analyse before designing
: profiling finds the current bottleneck; complexity analysis prevents future ones from being designed in.
